page: Amazon Web Services m3.2xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance running on the Xen hypervisor. It features an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor operating at 2.5 GHz, delivering 8 vCPUs across 4 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. The instance is equipped with 30.0 GB of system memory, providing 3.75 GB per core. Local storage is bundled as a 160 GB NVMe SSD, and network connectivity is rated at a baseline speed of 1 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate a clear division between single-core and multi-core capabilities. Single-core performance is weak, as evidenced by a stress-ng single-core score of 1279.49 and a Geekbench single-core score of 710.0. However, multi-core execution scales to average levels, with a stress-ng multi-core score of 6709.91 and a Passmark CPU score of 7144.84. Memory bandwidth remains average, measuring 49.24 GB/sec for uncached large-block reads. Cryptographic operations perform at average levels with a Passmark encryption score of 3100.82, aided by hardware-accelerated AES support, though the architecture lacks AVX-512 and SHA extensions.

Given its hardware profile, the m3.2xlarge is suited for general-purpose workloads such as static web serving, where it achieves an average throughput of 6.7 GB/sec, and basic database operations. From a resource density perspective, the instance offers a legacy hardware profile that provides a stable option for steady-state workloads that do not require modern CPU instruction sets. It is less suitable for modern compute-intensive tasks, single-threaded applications, or large-scale language model inference, where text generation speeds drop to a poor 5.26 tokens/sec for a 7B model.
description: Amazon Web Services m3.2xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor with 8 vCPUs, 4 physical cores, and 30.0 GB of RAM. Running on the Xen hypervisor, this x86_64 instance includes a bundled 160 GB NVMe SSD and a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Benchmark data shows weak single-core performance, with a Geekbench single-core score of 710.0, but average multi-core capabilities, as reflected in its Passmark CPU score of 7144.84. Memory bandwidth and static web serving throughput of 6.7 GB/sec also rank in the average tier. Lacking modern instruction sets like AVX-512, the instance is best suited for legacy general-purpose applications, static web serving, and moderate database operations rather than high-performance computing or large language model inference.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m3.2xlarge is a general-purpose instance with 8 Intel Xeon vCPUs, 30 GB RAM, and a 160 GB NVMe SSD, offering balanced multi-core performance for standard workloads.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m3.2xlarge is an 8-vCPU general-purpose instance with 30 GB RAM, a 160 GB NVMe SSD, and average multi-core benchmark performance.
tagline: A balanced general-purpose virtual server featuring dedicated legacy Intel Xeon cores, local NVMe storage, and steady multi-core performance for standard workloads.
bullet_points: Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor with 8 vCPUs and 30.0 GB RAM
Bundled 160 GB local NVMe SSD storage
Baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps on Xen hypervisor
Average multi-core performance suitable for static web serving and basic database tasks
Weak single-core performance and lack of modern AVX-512 instruction sets
